# 11. Getting Started with OpenAPI in Code-First Approach – Part 3

## 1. Purpose of This Lecture

This lecture completes the **Code-First workflow** by showing how to:

* Convert tested API history into an **OpenAPI Specification**
* Export that specification to **SwaggerHub**
* Understand what the generated OpenAPI document contains
* Recognize **limitations** of auto-generated specs
* Learn when and why **manual enhancement** is required

At the end of this lecture, you will have a **working OpenAPI document** generated from existing APIs.

---

## 2. Starting Point: API History in SwaggerHub Explorer

From the previous lecture:

* Multiple APIs were tested
* Requests and responses are now visible under the **History** tab in **SwaggerHub Explorer**

This history is the **raw input** used to generate the OpenAPI Specification.

---

## 3. Generating OpenAPI Specification from History

### 3.1 Selecting APIs

Steps:

1. Go to **History**
2. Select **all tested APIs**
3. Click **Add to Space**

You are then asked to provide:

* **Space name** (e.g. `demo`)
* **API name** (e.g. `demo`)

These names are arbitrary and can be changed later.

After confirmation:

* All selected APIs are added to the specified space

---

### 3.2 Verifying the Space

* Navigate to **Spaces**
* Confirm the new space (e.g. `demo`) exists
* Open the space
* Verify that all tested APIs appear under the API name

This confirms that SwaggerHub Explorer has successfully grouped your API history.

---

## 4. Exporting APIs to SwaggerHub

### 4.1 Why Export Is Required

SwaggerHub Explorer is used for:

* Testing APIs
* Discovering request/response structures

But:

* **OpenAPI specifications must be stored somewhere**
* That place is **SwaggerHub**

SwaggerHub acts like:

* GitHub → for source code
* SwaggerHub → for OpenAPI specifications

---

### 4.2 Export Process

Steps:

1. Click **Export API to SwaggerHub**
2. Provide API details:

    * API name (e.g. `demo`)
    * API version (e.g. `1.0`)
    * Owner (e.g. organization or username)
    * Visibility → **Public**

⚠️ **Important Note from Lecture**

* Private visibility requires a paid plan
* Trial users must use **Public**

After confirmation:

* A success message appears
* A link is provided to view the API definition in SwaggerHub

---

## 5. Viewing the Generated OpenAPI Specification

Once exported:

* Open **SwaggerHub**
* Locate the API via:

    * Direct link
    * Search / filters if needed
* Open the API definition

You will now see:

* A **YAML-based OpenAPI document**
* Generated automatically from API traffic

This is your **initial OpenAPI Specification**.

---

## 6. Swagger Editor & Swagger UI in Action

Inside SwaggerHub, two key tools appear together:

### 6.1 Swagger Editor (Left Side)

* Shows the OpenAPI document in **YAML**
* Performs **live validation**
* Highlights syntax and structural errors

Example:

* Changing `openapi:` to `api:` immediately throws an error

This prevents invalid OpenAPI documents.

---

### 6.2 Swagger UI (Right Side)

* Visual representation of the same specification
* Shows:

    * Server URLs
    * Endpoints
    * HTTP methods
    * Parameters
    * Request bodies
    * Responses
    * Schemas

This UI is what **consumers actually use** to understand your APIs.

![Image](https://static1.smartbear.co/swagger/media/blog/wp/difference-window.png)

![Image](https://static1.smartbear.co/swagger/media/images/tools/opensource/swaggerhub-swaggerui.png)

![Image](https://static1.smartbear.co/swagger/media/blog/swg-editor-error.png)

---

## 7. Value for API Consumers

With Swagger UI:

* Consumers do **not** need to read YAML
* They can:

    * Explore endpoints
    * Inspect parameters
    * View required fields
    * Understand response formats

For example:

* POST APIs show required fields (`name`, `job`)
* Field types are clearly visible
* Expected HTTP status codes are documented

This eliminates:

* Manual explanations
* Emails
* Meetings
* Guesswork

---

## 8. Important Limitation of Generated OpenAPI Specs

The instructor clearly highlights that:

> The generated OpenAPI specification is **not 100% production-ready**

### 8.1 Hardcoded Path Problem

Example:

* `/planets/1`
* `/planets/2`
* `/planets/3`

SwaggerHub Explorer treats these as **separate APIs**

But in reality, this should be:

* `/planets/{planetId}`

With:

* A path parameter
* Proper data type
* Description

---

### 8.2 What Explorer Cannot Infer

SwaggerHub Explorer:

* Captures **observed traffic**
* Cannot infer:

    * Generic path variables
    * Business rules
    * Authentication logic
    * Authorization constraints
    * Edge-case validations

This is why **manual refinement is mandatory** for serious projects.

---

## 9. How to Properly Enhance the Specification

To enhance the generated OpenAPI spec, you must understand:

* OpenAPI elements:

    * `openapi`
    * `info`
    * `servers`
    * `paths`
    * `parameters`
    * `requestBody`
    * `responses`
    * `schemas`
* The **importance of each element**
* How to convert hardcoded paths into generic paths
* How to add validations and descriptions

This knowledge is taught in upcoming lectures under the **Design-First approach**.

---

## 10. Transition to Design-First Approach

Next phase of the course:

* Assume **no APIs exist**
* Write OpenAPI Specification **from scratch**
* Learn every OpenAPI element deeply
* Use that knowledge to:

    * Design APIs properly
    * Enhance Code-First generated specs
    * Produce production-ready contracts

---

## 11. SwaggerHub as a Collaboration Platform

SwaggerHub allows:

* Creating new APIs
* Creating domains
* Creating templates
* Importing existing OpenAPI specs
* Team collaboration
* Versioning
* Centralized API governance

This is especially useful for:

* Medium to large organizations
* Teams working on multiple APIs

---

## 12. Instructor’s Practical Advice

* **Do not write OpenAPI from scratch** in Code-First scenarios
* Use SwaggerHub Explorer to:

    * Test deployed APIs
    * Generate a baseline OpenAPI spec
* Then:

    * Refine
    * Enhance
    * Standardize

Your fingers will thank you.

---

## 13. Practice Recommendation

You are encouraged to:

* Test APIs yourself
* Check API history
* Generate OpenAPI specs
* Validate them using:

    * Swagger Editor
    * Swagger UI

This hands-on practice builds real confidence with Swagger tools.

---

## 14. Additional Important Note

**(Added by me – not mentioned in the lecture)**

> In real production environments, teams often **lock auto-generated OpenAPI specs behind code reviews**, ensuring that inferred paths, schemas, and responses are normalized before publishing them to consumers.
